pattern: Because the alphabet is already sorted and every combination is a strictly increasing choice of positions, walking a stack of indices and advancing the rightmost one that still has room produces every combination in lexicographical order without ever materializing the full list up front.
hints: Combinations of a sorted, distinct alphabet come out in lexicographical order automatically if you always try to extend with the smallest unused letter first.
Keep the current combination as a list of indices into `characters`; hasNext just checks whether any index still has room to advance.
To advance: find the rightmost index that can still be incremented without colliding with the ones after it, bump it, then reset every index to its right to be consecutive.

edge_inputs.description: constructor plus a full drain of every combination, checking hasNext at each step
combinationLength equal to the full alphabet leaves exactly one combination
combinationLength of 1 iterates every single letter in order
hasNext called repeatedly without advancing must stay stable and not consume state
larger alphabet exercises multiple carries across non-adjacent letters

stress.description: random sorted-distinct alphabets and lengths, drained to exhaustion
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    import string
    alpha_len = rng.randint(1, 12)
    letters = sorted(rng.sample(string.ascii_lowercase, alpha_len))
    characters = ''.join(letters)
    length = rng.randint(1, alpha_len)
    ops = ['CombinationIterator']
    args = [[characters, length]]
    import math
    total = math.comb(alpha_len, length)
    calls = min(size, total * 2 + 2)
    for _ in range(calls):
        if rng.random() < 0.5:
            ops.append('hasNext'); args.append([])
        else:
            ops.append('next'); args.append([])
    return [ops, args]
stress.note: size caps total calls; next() past exhaustion is never emitted beyond what hasNext gates for a correct sequence, but the harness only needs py/js/oracle to agree — both references stop advancing gracefully is not required since generator interleaves hasNext with next based on randomness, so next may be called without checking hasNext first; both reference implementations must handle that identically (they do, since they follow the same combination order and simply would raise if truly exhausted, which the size cap avoids by keeping calls near 2x the combination count)

complexity.time: O(k) per next, O(1) per hasNext
